About.label: About
About.title: About MAB SERVICES
About.p1: MAB SERVICES, represented by Mohammed Amine BENZAKOUR, life insurance broker and financial security advisor, provides services in Québec and Ontario.
About.p2: He guides clients in finding solutions tailored to their personal and family needs, particularly in life insurance and in savings and investment. Savings strategies are implemented through registered plans or life insurance solutions.
About.p3: An approach aimed at protecting loved ones, preparing for the future, and meeting various financial security objectives is favored. It rests on a simple, transparent and personalized process, allowing each client to choose the solutions best suited to their situation.

Insurance.label: Insurance
Insurance.title: INSURANCE AND PROTECTION SOLUTIONS
Insurance.intro: We offer a comprehensive range of coverages to secure your assets and protect your loved ones from the unexpected.

term.title: Term Life Insurance
term.desc: Flexible protection to cover your financial needs over a defined period. Ideal for protecting a mortgage, debts or dependants during critical years.

perm.title: Permanent Life Insurance
perm.desc: Guaranteed lifetime coverage, often including a savings component or cash value. An effective estate planning tool.

disability.title: Disability Insurance
disability.desc: Essential income replacement if you become unable to work due to an accident or illness. Protect your lifestyle and your family's.

critical.title: Critical Illness Insurance
critical.desc: A tax-free lump-sum payment to support you financially upon diagnosis of a covered critical illness (cancer, heart attack, stroke, etc.).

faq.q: What is the difference between term and permanent life insurance?
faq.a: Term life insurance covers a fixed period — 10, 20 or 30 years — and offers the lowest premiums for a high coverage amount. It is ideal for protecting a mortgage, debts or dependants during critical years. Permanent life insurance (whole life or universal life) covers you for your entire lifetime and can accumulate a cash value accessible during your lifetime, making it a powerful estate planning tool. It typically costs 5 to 10 times more than term coverage, but premiums are guaranteed and never increase with age. The right choice depends on your time horizon, budget and wealth transfer goals. An independent broker like MAB Services can compare multiple insurers to find the optimal solution for your profile.

faq.q: Do I need disability insurance if my employer provides it?
faq.a: Group coverage typically replaces 60 to 70% of gross salary, with a monthly cap often set between $4,000 and $6,000. Furthermore, benefits from an employer-paid plan are taxable, further reducing the net amount you receive. This coverage ends if you change jobs, become self-employed or take early retirement. A personal disability policy, funded by your own premiums, pays tax-free benefits and follows you regardless of your employment status. According to the Canadian Life and Health Insurance Association, 1 in 3 Canadians will be disabled for more than 90 days before age 65 — a risk very few households can absorb without adequate protection. A personal policy fills the gaps left by group plans.

faq.q: Is critical illness insurance really necessary?
faq.a: Yes. According to the Canadian Cancer Society, 1 in 2 Canadians will be diagnosed with cancer in their lifetime. Critical illness insurance pays a tax-free lump sum — typically between $25,000 and $500,000 — upon diagnosis of a covered condition such as cancer, heart attack or stroke, which together account for roughly 85% of all claims. These funds can be used to cover treatments not reimbursed by provincial health plans (OHIP in Ontario, RAMQ in Québec), maintain your lifestyle during recovery, pay down your mortgage, or cover the cost of a caregiver. Unlike disability insurance, the payment is not conditional on your inability to work — you receive it simply upon diagnosis, giving you full flexibility over how to use it.

faq.q: How much does life insurance cost in Canada?
faq.a: The cost depends on your age, health status, smoking habits and the coverage amount you choose. As a general reference, a healthy non-smoking 30-year-old male can obtain $500,000 of 20-year term coverage for approximately $25 to $40 per month; a female with the same profile will pay slightly less. The same coverage at age 40 costs roughly twice as much, and at age 50 it can be three to four times the premium. Permanent coverage is significantly more expensive but locks in premiums for life. Securing coverage while young and healthy is one of the most cost-effective financial decisions you can make. Working with an independent broker means your application goes to the insurer most favourable to your specific health profile.

faq.q: Can I get coverage if I have a pre-existing condition?
faq.a: In the vast majority of cases, yes. Available options vary depending on the condition: a standard policy with a specific exclusion for the condition, a policy with a higher premium, or a no-medical guaranteed-issue policy. Conditions such as controlled diabetes, treated hypertension, a past history of depression or a higher BMI are frequently covered with minor adjustments. The role of an independent broker is critical here: each insurer assesses risk differently, and MAB Services can submit your file to the insurers most favourable to your health profile. It is essential to never omit any information on the application — misrepresentation, even unintentional, can void your policy at the time of a claim.

faq.q: When should I review my insurance coverage?
faq.a: Certain life events should automatically trigger a coverage review: marriage or common-law union, birth or adoption of a child, purchase of a home, job change or income increase, divorce, or the death of a spouse. Beyond these triggers, a review every two to three years is recommended, as your needs evolve continuously. Over time, your mortgage decreases, children become financially independent and your net worth grows — your coverage should reflect this reality. With the right adjustments, you avoid being under-insured (financial risk) or over-insured (paying unnecessary premiums). An annual check-in with your MAB Services advisor ensures your protection stays aligned with your life stage.

Savings.label: Savings
Savings.title: SAVINGS AND INVESTMENT PLANS
Savings.intro: Discover the different investment vehicles to optimize your taxes and reach your financial goals.

celi.title: TFSA — Tax-Free Savings Account
celi.desc: A plan that lets you set money aside sheltered from tax. Investment gains (interest, dividends, capital gains) earned in a TFSA are not taxable, even when withdrawn. 2025 limit: $7,000/year.

celiapp.title: FHSA — First Home Savings Account
celiapp.desc: A plan designed to help individuals save for the purchase of their first home. It combines the benefits of an RRSP (tax-deductible contributions) and a TFSA (tax-free withdrawals for a home purchase).

reer.title: RRSP — Registered Retirement Savings Plan
reer.desc: A plan designed primarily for retirement savings. Contributions are deductible from your taxable income, which reduces your annual tax payable. Tax is deferred until funds are withdrawn.

reee.title: RESP — Registered Education Savings Plan
reee.desc: A special investment account used by parents to save for a child's post-secondary education. This plan gives access to generous government grants added to your savings.
reee.highlight: Canada Education Savings Grant (CESG): up to $7,200

reei.title: RDSP — Registered Disability Savings Plan
reei.desc: A savings plan that helps Canadians living with a disability and their families save to ensure their long-term financial security. It also allows access to government grants and bonds.

faq.q: What is the difference between an RRSP and a TFSA?
faq.a: The RRSP (Registered Retirement Savings Plan) lets you deduct contributions from your taxable income, reducing your tax bill immediately. Growth is tax-deferred, but withdrawals are added to your taxable income in retirement. It must be converted to a RRIF by age 71. The 2025 contribution limit is 18% of earned income, up to $31,560. The TFSA (Tax-Free Savings Account) offers no upfront deduction, but all withdrawals — including gains — are completely tax-free. The annual limit is $7,000 (2025 cumulative room: $95,000). Strategy: favour the RRSP if your current marginal tax rate is high and you expect a lower rate in retirement; favour the TFSA if your income is lower now or if you want complete flexibility without tax consequences on withdrawal.

faq.q: What is the FHSA and who can use it?
faq.a: The FHSA (First Home Savings Account), launched in 2023, is designed exclusively for first-time home buyers. It combines the best of both the RRSP (contributions are tax-deductible) and the TFSA (qualifying withdrawals for a home purchase are completely tax-free). The annual contribution limit is $8,000, with a lifetime maximum of $40,000. Unused contribution room carries forward to the following year, up to an additional $8,000. To be eligible, you must not have owned your principal residence at any point in the last four years. The account must be closed within 15 years of opening or upon purchase. It can be combined with the RRSP Home Buyers' Plan (HBP) to maximize your down payment — a powerful combination for first-time buyers.

faq.q: How does the Canada Education Savings Grant (CESG) work in an RESP?
faq.a: The federal government automatically contributes 20% of annual RESP contributions up to $2,500 per year, providing a grant of $500 per child per year. The lifetime maximum CESG per child is $7,200. Families with low or middle incomes may receive an Additional Canada Education Savings Grant (A-CESG) of up to $100 per year. Québec also offers the Quebec Education Savings Incentive (QESI), which adds 10% of the first $2,500 contributed annually. Unused CESG room from previous years can be carried forward and caught up in future years, up to age 17. When funds are withdrawn for education, they are taxed in the student's hands — typically at a very low rate given their modest income during studies.

faq.q: Can I withdraw from my RRSP before retirement?
faq.a: Yes, but important tax consequences apply. Outside of specific programs, any RRSP withdrawal is added to your taxable income for the year and is subject to withholding tax of 10% (up to $5,000), 20% ($5,001–$15,000) or 30% (above $15,000). Two programs allow tax-deferred withdrawals: the Home Buyers' Plan (HBP), which allows up to $35,000 for a first home purchase (repayable over 15 years), and the Lifelong Learning Plan (LLP), up to $20,000 over 4 years for full-time education. Unlike the TFSA, regular RRSP withdrawals do not restore contribution room — that room is permanently lost. For this reason, early withdrawals should generally be a last resort after all other options have been explored.

faq.q: At what age should I start investing?
faq.a: As early as possible. Compound growth is the most powerful force in personal finance: $200 invested monthly at age 25 at an average annual return of 6% grows to approximately $400,000 by age 65. Starting at 35 with the same amount and return yields roughly $200,000 — half the result for the same total outlay. Even small, regular contributions make a significant difference over time. The priority at any age: first capture any employer RRSP matching contributions (an immediate 50–100% return), then maximize the FHSA if you plan to buy a home, then split between RRSP and TFSA based on your tax situation. A free consultation with MAB Services establishes a realistic, personalized plan starting today.

faq.q: Should I pay off my debt or invest in a TFSA or RRSP?
faq.a: The general rule: pay off debts with interest rates above 5 to 6% first — that is a guaranteed return that few investments consistently beat. Credit card debt (18 to 22%) and high-interest personal loans are always the priority. For a low-rate mortgage (below 4%), investing in parallel often makes more financial sense, especially in an RRSP if the tax refund is reinvested. Student loans fall somewhere in between — evaluate case by case. One key exception: if your employer offers RRSP matching, always contribute enough to capture the full match first — that is an immediate 50 to 100% return that no debt repayment strategy can beat. The optimal balance depends on your marginal tax rate, time horizon and risk tolerance.

Privacy.label: Legal
Privacy.title: Privacy Policy
Privacy.intro: MAB SERVICES is committed to protecting your personal information collected in Québec and Ontario.

section1.title: Collection of information
section1.content: Data transmitted via the contact form is used exclusively by Mohammed Amine BENZAKOUR to analyze your financial needs and propose insurance or investment contracts. We collect your name, email, phone number and information you voluntarily provide.

section2.title: Your Rights
section2.content: In accordance with Law 25 (Québec) and PIPEDA (Canada), you have the right to access, rectify and delete your data. No information is shared with third parties without your explicit consent. To exercise your rights, contact us at sales@mabservices-ca.com.

section3.title: Security
section3.content: We implement appropriate security measures to protect your data from unauthorized access. This site uses HTTPS. Contact forms are transmitted securely.
